_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
condition: Arthroscopic Meniscus Repair / Meniscectomy, Reconstruction of Cruciate Ligament with implant and brace
min_doctor_qualification: Diploma in Orthopedics with 10 years of experience
clinical_indications: When meniscal repair is not possible, partial resection of the meniscus is indicated

preauth.label: Clinical notes with history, signs, symptoms, evaluation findings, indication for procedure, planned line of management and advice for admission
MRI scan of affected part labelled with patient ID, date and side (Left/Right)

claim.label: Intra operative still image with patient name

cpd_claim.q: Does the intra operative still image with patient name submitted in Arthroscopic Meniscus Repair/ Meniscectomy patients?

common_queries: Intra operative still image with patient name is missing or does not contain patient name for Arthroscopic Meniscus Repair/Meniscectomy patients.
MRI scan of affected part is not labelled with patient ID, date, or side (Left/Right).
Clinical photograph of affected part is missing.

clinical_key_pointers: The meniscus has functions in load bearing, load transmission, shock absorption, joint stability, joint lubrication, and joint congruity.

Total meniscectomy: Nowadays, total meniscectomy is not a common procedure. However, previously it was a popular procedure, and short-term outcome results of this technique were regarded as excellent.
Partial meniscectomy: When meniscal repair is not possible, partial resection of the meniscus is indicated.
Repair: Meniscal repair has evolved from open to arthroscopic techniques, which include the inside-out and outside-in suture repairs and the all-inside techniques.

Open repair: Open meniscal repair offers the advantage of better preparation of the tear site. However, only the most peripheral of tears in the red-red zone are amenable to this technique because of exposure and accessibility.
Arthroscopic inside-out repair: The inside-out meniscal repair technique involves fixation of a tear by placing sutures from inside the knee to a protected area on the outside of the joint capsule.

Arthroscopic outside-in repair: Outside-in techniques involve passing sutures percutaneously through spinal needles at the joint line across the tear, and then retrieving the sutures intra-articularly under arthroscopic observation.
